sections.plain_summary: Depression is a common but often poorly understood mental health condition involving persistent low mood, loss of interest, fatigue, and a range of physical symptoms that many people don't associate with it. It has biological, psychological, and social roots — and for a significant number of people, underlying physical factors such as inflammation, hormonal imbalance, gut dysfunction, or nutritional deficiencies play a central role that mainstream treatment often misses.
sections.detailed_summary:
Depression — clinically termed Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) when severe, or Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD, formerly dysthymia) when long-lasting — is characterised by a sustained change in mood, motivation, cognition, and physical functioning. It is one of the most common disabling conditions worldwide, yet it remains widely undertreated, misdiagnosed, or treated in ways that address symptoms without investigating root causes.

The conventional model frames depression primarily as a serotonin deficiency correctable with SSRIs. This is an oversimplification. Current evidence points to multiple overlapping mechanisms: chronic low-grade inflammation, dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, disrupted circadian rhythms, gut-brain axis dysfunction, mitochondrial dysfunction, nutritional deficiencies (particularly B vitamins, magnesium, zinc, omega-3s, and vitamin D), thyroid abnormalities, and unresolved trauma or chronic stress.

Many people with depression have seen standard prescribers and received antidepressants that provide partial relief at best — or none. This often reflects the fact that the biological driver has not been identified. A subset of people with apparent 'treatment-resistant depression' improve substantially once an underlying physical contributor is identified and addressed. Self-directed research into potential root causes is a legitimate and often necessary part of this process.

prevalence.summary: Depression affects roughly 1 in 6 people at some point in their lifetime and is one of the leading causes of disability globally.
prevalence.estimate: 5–7% of adults at any given time; lifetime prevalence approximately 15–20%
prevalence.notes: Prevalence is likely underestimated due to underdiagnosis, stigma, and variation in diagnostic criteria. Women are diagnosed roughly twice as often as men, though men may underreport. Rates are higher in people with chronic physical illness, trauma history, ADHD, and certain autoimmune conditions. The COVID-19 pandemic led to a documented spike in rates.

subgroups.name: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
subgroups.description: Discrete episodes of severe depression lasting at least two weeks, often with full recovery between episodes but significant impairment during them.
distinguishing_features: Episodes typically last weeks to months
Full or near-full recovery between episodes is common
May be single-episode or recurrent
Suicidal ideation more likely during acute episodes

subgroups.name: Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD / Dysthymia)
subgroups.description: A lower-grade but chronic form of depression lasting at least two years, often experienced as 'always having been this way'.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms are less severe but highly persistent
Often missed because people normalise chronic low mood
Higher risk of developing MDD episodes on top of baseline (double depression)
Often has earlier onset than MDD

subgroups.name: Atypical Depression
subgroups.description: A subtype characterised by mood reactivity (mood brightens in response to positive events) alongside specific symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Mood can temporarily lift in response to good news or pleasant events
Hypersomnia (sleeping too much) rather than insomnia
Increased appetite and carbohydrate cravings
Leaden paralysis — heavy, weighted feeling in limbs
Longstanding pattern of rejection sensitivity

subgroups.name: Melancholic Depression
subgroups.description: A biologically severe subtype with prominent physical features and less response to psychological interventions alone.
distinguishing_features: Profound anhedonia — inability to feel pleasure even briefly
Depression is worst in the morning
Early morning awakening
Psychomotor retardation or agitation
Strong biological component; more likely to require medication

subgroups.name: Inflammatory / Immune-Mediated Depression
subgroups.description: A subtype increasingly recognised in research where chronic inflammation drives depressive symptoms, often seen in people with autoimmune disease, obesity, or chronic infection.
distinguishing_features: Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha) on testing
Strong fatigue, cognitive slowing, and social withdrawal ('sickness behaviour')
Often less responsive to standard antidepressants
May improve with anti-inflammatory interventions
Frequently co-occurs with inflammatory physical conditions

subgroups.name: Postpartum Depression (PPD)
subgroups.description: Depression occurring in the weeks to months after childbirth, driven by a combination of hormonal shifts, sleep deprivation, and psychosocial stress.
distinguishing_features: Onset within 4 weeks to 12 months postpartum
Distinct from 'baby blues' which resolve within two weeks
May include intrusive thoughts about the baby
Hormonal factors (estrogen and progesterone crash) are central
Often underreported due to stigma around new parenthood

subgroups.name: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)
subgroups.description: A seasonal pattern of depression, most commonly occurring in autumn and winter, linked to reduced light exposure and disrupted circadian signalling.
distinguishing_features: Predictable seasonal onset and remission
Hypersomnia and increased appetite typical (similar to atypical subtype)
Strong response to bright light therapy in many people
More common at higher latitudes
May persist as milder 'subsyndromal SAD' in others

subgroups.name: Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD)
subgroups.description: Depression that has not responded adequately to at least two different antidepressant trials. Often reflects an unidentified biological driver rather than true drug resistance.
distinguishing_features: Failure to respond to standard antidepressants
Warrants thorough investigation for underlying physical causes
Inflammatory, metabolic, hormonal, and nutritional factors frequently implicated
May respond to non-standard approaches: ketamine, TMS, ECT, or root-cause treatment
Often has significant comorbidity burden

symptoms.symptom: Persistent low, sad, or empty mood
symptoms.notes: Present most of the day, most days. May be experienced as numbness or emptiness rather than sadness in some individuals.

symptoms.symptom: Loss of interest or pleasure in previously enjoyed activities (anhedonia)
symptoms.notes: One of the two core diagnostic symptoms. Can affect hobbies, relationships, food, and sex.
Often disproportionate to activity. Present even after adequate sleep. A very common presenting complaint.
Early morning awakening is classic for melancholic depression; sleeping too much is more typical of atypical or SAD subtypes.

symptoms.symptom: Cognitive impairment — poor concentration, memory problems, mental fog
symptoms.notes: Often described as 'brain fog'. Can significantly impair work performance. May persist even when mood improves.

symptoms.symptom: Psychomotor changes — slowing down or agitation
symptoms.notes: Objective slowing of movement, speech, and thought; or conversely, restlessness and inability to sit still.
Can go either way: significant weight loss from lack of appetite, or weight gain from emotional eating or carbohydrate cravings.

symptoms.symptom: Feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt
symptoms.notes: Often disproportionate to actual circumstances. Can take on a ruminative quality.
A key risk factor for suicidal ideation. Distinct from low mood — involves a specific belief that things cannot improve.

symptoms.symptom: Recurrent thoughts of death or suicide
symptoms.notes: Ranges from passive ('I wouldn't mind not waking up') to active planning. Any suicidal ideation warrants professional assessment.
More commonly the presenting face of depression in men and adolescents than classic sadness.

symptoms.symptom: Physical pain — headaches, joint pain, back pain
symptoms.notes: Depression and pain share neural pathways. Many people present to primary care with physical pain and are never screened for depression.
The gut-brain axis is bidirectional; gut symptoms are common in depression and often overlooked as part of the same condition.
Avoiding friends, family, and social situations. Often a consequence that also perpetuates the depression.
Can be caused by depression itself or by antidepressant medication (particularly SSRIs/SNRIs).
Some people feel neither sadness nor joy — a flat, disconnected state. Can also be a side effect of antidepressant medication.
Diurnal variation — symptoms worst in the morning and improving slightly through the day — is a hallmark of melancholic depression and HPA axis dysregulation.

key_patterns: Low thyroid function (hypothyroidism) presenting as depression, fatigue, and cognitive slowing — full thyroid panel including TSH, Free T3, Free T4, and thyroid antibodies
Elevated inflammatory markers (hs-CRP, IL-6, ferritin) alongside depression and fatigue, suggesting inflammatory subtype
Low vitamin D levels (below 50 nmol/L) — common in depressed populations, especially in low-sunlight environments
Iron deficiency or anaemia without overt anaemia (ferritin below 30–50 µg/L) causing fatigue and low mood
B12 and folate deficiency — neurological symptoms, mood disturbance, and cognitive impairment
Low testosterone (in men and women) or oestrogen dysregulation correlating with mood and motivation symptoms
Elevated cortisol or dysregulated cortisol rhythm (via salivary cortisol testing) suggesting HPA axis dysfunction
Blood sugar dysregulation — reactive hypoglycaemia or insulin resistance causing mood fluctuations tied to meals
Coeliac disease or significant gluten sensitivity presenting with neuropsychiatric symptoms
Omega-3 index below 8% — associated with increased depression risk and inflammatory status

at_home.name: PHQ-9 mood self-assessment
at_home.description: A validated nine-question screening tool for depression severity. Freely available online. Tracks core symptoms over the past two weeks and scores them on severity. Useful for establishing a baseline and monitoring change over time.
at_home.safety_notes: A screening tool, not a diagnosis. High scores warrant professional assessment. The PHQ-9 does not identify cause — only presence and severity of symptoms.

at_home.name: Symptom and mood journal
at_home.description: Tracking mood, sleep, energy, appetite, exercise, and key stressors daily in a simple journal or app. Over two to four weeks, patterns often emerge — such as mood tied to sleep quality, menstrual cycle phase, particular foods, or sunlight exposure. This data is valuable for any clinician visit.
at_home.safety_notes: Excessive self-monitoring can increase rumination in some people. If journalling worsens mood, brief structured entries are preferable to detailed reflection.

at_home.name: Sleep quality assessment
at_home.description: Tracking sleep onset, duration, and waking using a journal or wearable device. Poor sleep both causes and perpetuates depression. Identifying specific patterns (early morning waking, difficulty getting to sleep, hypersomnia) helps narrow subtype and guide intervention.
at_home.safety_notes: Sleep tracking apps can increase anxiety about sleep quality in some people. Use as information, not as a performance metric.

at_home.name: Light exposure log (for possible SAD)
at_home.description: Noting daily outdoor light exposure and correlating with mood across seasons. If mood consistently worsens in autumn and winter and improves in spring or summer, SAD should be considered. Some people trial a 10,000-lux light therapy lamp (used for 20–30 minutes in the morning) as a low-risk self-experiment.
at_home.safety_notes: Light therapy can trigger hypomania in people with undiagnosed bipolar disorder. If there is any history of unusually elevated mood, grandiosity, or reduced need for sleep, consult a clinician before trialling light therapy.

at_home.name: Dietary pattern review
at_home.description: A detailed review of diet quality, meal timing, and blood sugar patterns. Ultra-processed food, high sugar intake, and irregular eating patterns are associated with worse depression outcomes. Noting whether mood fluctuates in relation to food intake may reveal reactive hypoglycaemia as a contributor.
at_home.safety_notes: Dietary restriction should not be undertaken impulsively in the context of depression — disordered eating can co-occur. Consult a dietitian if eating habits are already a source of distress.

clinical.name: Full thyroid panel
clinical.description: TSH, Free T3, Free T4, and thyroid antibodies (TPO and TgAb). Hypothyroidism and subclinical hypothyroidism can be clinically indistinguishable from depression. Standard GP tests often only include TSH, which may miss early dysfunction.
clinical.availability: Available via GP; full panel more reliably obtained through functional medicine practitioners or private blood testing services.

clinical.name: Full blood count and iron panel
clinical.description: Detects anaemia and iron deficiency, which cause fatigue, poor concentration, and low mood. Ferritin is the most sensitive marker — levels below 30–50 µg/L may be symptomatic even without frank anaemia.
clinical.availability: Available via GP. Ferritin is routinely testable but may not be included unless specifically requested.

clinical.name: Vitamin D (25-OH-D)
clinical.description: Low vitamin D is consistently associated with depression in large population studies. Causality is debated, but deficiency is common and correctable. Levels below 50 nmol/L are considered deficient in most guidelines.
clinical.availability: Available via GP (may require justification) or private testing. Home finger-prick tests are widely available and reliable.

clinical.name: Vitamin B12 and folate
clinical.description: Both B12 and folate are essential for neurotransmitter synthesis and methylation. Deficiency can present with depression, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and neurological symptoms. Active B12 (holotranscobalamin) is a more sensitive marker than serum B12.
clinical.availability: Available via GP. Active B12 may require private testing.

clinical.name: High-sensitivity CRP and inflammatory markers
clinical.description: hs-CRP, and where available IL-6 and TNF-alpha, help identify the inflammatory subtype of depression. CRP above 1 mg/L in a depressed individual suggests immune activation as a potential driver.
clinical.availability: hs-CRP available via GP or private testing. IL-6 and TNF-alpha more typically through research or specialist settings.

clinical.name: Sex hormone panel
clinical.description: Testosterone (total and free), oestradiol, progesterone, LH, FSH, and SHBG. Low testosterone in men and perimenopausal hormonal shifts in women are common and correctable drivers of depressive symptoms. Standard depression care rarely investigates hormones.
clinical.availability: Available via GP (with justification) or private testing. Hormone specialist (endocrinologist or gynaecologist) for interpretation and treatment.

clinical.name: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, and fasting insulin
clinical.description: Insulin resistance and blood sugar dysregulation are associated with depression risk and may worsen outcomes. HbA1c reflects average blood glucose over 12 weeks; fasting insulin is a sensitive marker of early insulin resistance that is not routinely ordered.
clinical.availability: HbA1c and fasting glucose via GP. Fasting insulin may require private testing.

clinical.name: Salivary cortisol (4-point or DUTCH panel)
clinical.description: Measures the diurnal cortisol rhythm — typically high in the morning and declining through the day. A flat or inverted rhythm indicates HPA axis dysregulation, which is common in chronic stress-related and melancholic depression. The DUTCH test also includes sex hormones and their metabolites.
clinical.availability: Not available through most GPs. Ordered by functional medicine doctors, integrative practitioners, or private labs.

clinical.name: Coeliac serology (tTG-IgA and total IgA)
clinical.description: Coeliac disease has well-documented neuropsychiatric manifestations including depression. All individuals with depression and any gastrointestinal symptoms or family history of coeliac disease should be screened. Must be tested while actively eating gluten.
clinical.availability: Available via GP. Requires gluten consumption in the weeks before testing for accurate results.

clinical.name: Structured psychiatric assessment (PHQ-9, MADRS, or HAM-D)
clinical.description: Clinician-administered tools that measure depression severity, track change over time, and distinguish depression from bipolar disorder, anxiety, or other conditions. These provide a baseline for treatment decisions.
clinical.availability: Administered by psychiatrists, psychologists, or trained GPs.

clinical.name: Genetic pharmacogenomic testing (e.g. GeneSight, Genomind)
clinical.description: Analyses genetic variants in drug-metabolising enzymes (CYP450 system) and neurotransmitter receptors to predict which antidepressants are likely to be effective or cause side effects. Can reduce trial-and-error prescribing significantly.
clinical.availability: Available privately. Some psychiatrists and integrative practitioners order this routinely; mainstream prescribers less commonly.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety and depression co-occur in approximately 50% of cases. They share overlapping neurobiology (HPA axis dysregulation, amygdala hyperactivity) and frequently require integrated treatment. One often masks or precedes the other.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Adverse childhood experiences and adult trauma are among the strongest known predictors of depression. Trauma dysregulates the stress response, alters brain structure, and drives chronic inflammation — all mechanisms implicated in depression. PTSD and depression frequently co-occur.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Depression and fibromyalgia share high comorbidity, overlapping inflammatory and central sensitisation mechanisms, and a bidirectional relationship where each can worsen the other.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism — including subclinical hypothyroidism — is a common and reversible cause of depressive symptoms. Thyroid function should be assessed in any person presenting with depression, particularly if fatigue and cognitive symptoms are prominent.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: IBS and depression co-occur far more often than chance would predict. The gut-brain axis is bidirectional: gut dysbiosis and inflammation can drive mood changes, while depression alters gut motility and permeability. Both conditions share central sensitisation mechanisms.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: The gut microbiome produces neurotransmitters, influences vagal signalling, and modulates immune activation — all relevant to depression. Dysbiosis (disrupted microbiome composition) is increasingly recognised as a potential contributor to depressive symptoms via the gut-brain axis.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Depression is associated with altered central nervous system pain processing. Central sensitisation — heightened neural reactivity — overlaps mechanistically with depression and is common in people with both depression and chronic pain.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: ADHD and depression co-occur frequently. ADHD often goes undiagnosed into adulthood and the chronic experience of underperformance, rejection sensitivity, and executive dysfunction can drive secondary depression. Conversely, depression can impair attention and be mistaken for ADHD.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction in POTS can produce low mood, brain fog, and fatigue that resembles or coexists with depression. Dysautonomia and depression share some autonomic nervous system overlap, and POTS is frequently missed as an underlying contributor to mood symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cell activation drives systemic inflammation and histamine release, both of which can affect mood and neurological function. MCAS is increasingly identified in people with treatment-resistant depression and multiple system symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Hormonal / menstrual cycle effects
related_conditions.explanation: Fluctuations in oestrogen and progesterone across the menstrual cycle, perimenopause, and postpartum period are major drivers of mood dysregulation. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) is a hormonally-driven depressive condition often misdiagnosed as general depression.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease has well-documented neuropsychiatric manifestations including depression. Undiagnosed coeliac disease causes chronic intestinal inflammation and nutrient malabsorption (B12, folate, iron, zinc) — all contributing to depressive pathology.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Elevated histamine can affect the central nervous system, contributing to anxiety, irritability, and low mood. Histamine intolerance is rarely investigated in people with depression and may be a contributing factor in a subset of individuals.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis is associated with significantly elevated rates of depression. Chronic pain, diagnostic delay, hormonal dysregulation, and systemic inflammation all contribute. Depression in endometriosis is often undertreated and attributed to pain alone.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and hypermobility spectrum disorder carry high rates of anxiety and depression, likely through a combination of chronic pain, mast cell activation, autonomic dysfunction, and the psychological burden of a poorly-recognised chronic illness.

related_conditions.name: Bipolar disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Bipolar disorder is frequently misdiagnosed as recurrent unipolar depression, particularly when hypomanic or manic episodes are mild or have not yet occurred. Antidepressants given without a mood stabiliser can trigger mixed states or rapid cycling in bipolar patients — making accurate diagnosis critical.

related_conditions.name: Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD)
related_conditions.explanation: PMDD produces severe depressive and emotional symptoms that are strictly tied to the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle and resolve after menstruation. It is often misdiagnosed as general depression and requires hormonal rather than purely antidepressant treatment.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome / ME-CFS
related_conditions.explanation: ME-CFS is frequently misattributed to depression due to overlapping fatigue and functional impairment. Key distinctions include post-exertional malaise, orthostatic intolerance, and the fact that psychiatric treatment alone does not help ME-CFS. The two can co-occur.

related_conditions.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Insulin resistance is associated with increased depression risk. Shared mechanisms include systemic inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and HPA axis dysregulation. Some researchers propose 'metabolic depression' as a distinct subtype.

strategies.name: Structured physical exercise
strategies.description: Aerobic exercise — particularly 30–45 minutes three to five times per week — has robust evidence for reducing depressive symptoms. It increases BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), modulates inflammation, improves sleep, and regulates the HPA axis. Resistance training also shows benefit. Exercise is comparable to antidepressants in mild-to-moderate depression in meta-analyses.
strategies.notes: Starting very small (even 10-minute walks) and building gradually is more effective than ambitious plans that collapse. Accountability (a class, a walking partner) significantly increases adherence.

strategies.name: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT)
strategies.description: CBT is one of the most evidence-based psychological treatments for depression. It targets distorted thinking patterns and behavioural avoidance that perpetuate low mood. It is as effective as antidepressants in mild-to-moderate depression and has lower relapse rates when discontinued.
strategies.notes: Quality varies significantly between therapists. Internet-delivered CBT (iCBT) programmes show meaningful benefit for those without access to in-person therapy.

strategies.name: Behavioural Activation
strategies.description: A structured approach from CBT that counters depression's tendency to withdraw by scheduling valued and rewarding activities. Particularly effective when motivation is very low because it acts before waiting for mood to improve — behaviour comes first, mood follows.
strategies.notes: Can be self-directed using workbooks. Starting with very small, easy activities and building gradually is essential.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene and circadian regulation
strategies.description: Consistent sleep and wake times, morning light exposure, reduced evening light and screen exposure, and avoiding alcohol all stabilise the circadian system. Disrupted circadian rhythms are a central mechanism in depression. Regularising sleep timing can produce rapid improvements in mood.
strategies.notes: Some people with depression sleep excessively — limiting sleep to a consistent window can be temporarily uncomfortable but improves mood regulation faster than permitting extended sleep.

strategies.name: Anti-inflammatory diet
strategies.description: A diet rich in vegetables, oily fish, legumes, nuts, whole grains, and olive oil (Mediterranean pattern) and low in ultra-processed foods, refined sugar, and industrial seed oils reduces systemic inflammation and supports gut microbiome diversity. Multiple randomised controlled trials (including the SMILES trial) show dietary improvement reduces depression scores significantly.
strategies.notes: Diet change is most impactful in those currently eating a poor-quality diet. It is not a replacement for psychological or pharmacological treatment in severe depression but is a meaningful adjunct.

strategies.name: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA-dominant)
strategies.description: EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) at doses of 1–2g per day has the strongest evidence among supplements for depression. It modulates inflammation, serotonin signalling, and membrane fluidity in neurons. Meta-analyses support benefit, particularly in people with elevated inflammation.
strategies.notes: EPA-dominant formulations (more EPA than DHA) are preferred based on trial evidence. Quality matters — choose fish oil with third-party purity testing. Takes 6–12 weeks to see full effect.

strategies.name: Antidepressant medication
strategies.description: SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, and other antidepressant classes are effective for moderate-to-severe depression in many people. They work best as part of a broader treatment plan. Response rates are approximately 40–60% for a first antidepressant, improving with sequential trials or combination approaches.
strategies.notes: Not identifying the biological driver (e.g. thyroid dysfunction, nutrient deficiency) means antidepressants may provide partial relief at best. Side effects — including emotional blunting and sexual dysfunction — are common and often not adequately discussed. Discontinuation should be done slowly under supervision.

strategies.name: Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT)
strategies.description: Combines mindfulness practices with cognitive therapy elements. Particularly effective at reducing relapse in people with three or more prior depressive episodes. Teaches the ability to observe thoughts without being captured by them.
strategies.notes: Less effective as an acute treatment for severe depression but highly effective as a maintenance strategy. Available as group programmes or through apps (e.g. Headspace, Woebot).

strategies.name: Light therapy (for SAD and circadian disruption)
strategies.description: A 10,000-lux bright light box used for 20–30 minutes upon waking is a first-line treatment for SAD. Evidence also supports its use as an adjunct in non-seasonal depression. Morning timing is critical — it advances the circadian phase and suppresses melatonin appropriately.
strategies.notes: Should be used with caution in anyone with potential bipolar disorder. Most benefit is seen within one to two weeks.

strategies.name: Social connection and structured social engagement
strategies.description: Social isolation both causes and perpetuates depression. Deliberately scheduling regular social contact — even when motivation is absent — is a behavioural intervention with strong evidence. Volunteering, group activities, and support groups all show benefit.
strategies.notes: Depression actively generates thoughts that social contact is pointless or unwanted. Acting against these thoughts (rather than waiting to feel motivated) is the key mechanism.

strategies.name: Vitamin D supplementation
strategies.description: In people with confirmed deficiency, vitamin D supplementation (typically 1,000–4,000 IU daily) may improve mood. Evidence is stronger for deficient populations than for those with normal levels.
strategies.notes: Test before supplementing to establish baseline. Co-supplementing with vitamin K2 and magnesium improves vitamin D utilisation.

strategies.name: Magnesium supplementation
strategies.description: Magnesium plays a role in NMDA receptor function, HPA axis regulation, and neurotransmitter synthesis. Deficiency is common in Western diets and in people under chronic stress. Observational data consistently links low magnesium to higher depression rates.
strategies.notes: Glycinate and malate forms are generally better tolerated than oxide. Takes several weeks to see effect. 200–400 mg daily is a typical dose range.

strategies.name: Ketamine and esketamine (for treatment-resistant depression)
strategies.description: Ketamine and its derivative esketamine (Spravato) produce rapid antidepressant effects — often within hours — through NMDA receptor antagonism and BDNF release. Used in treatment-resistant depression and acute suicidal crises.
strategies.notes: Effects are often temporary without maintenance dosing. Administered in controlled clinical settings. Not appropriate as a first-line treatment but represents a meaningful option when standard treatments have failed.

strategies.name: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS)
strategies.description: A non-invasive brain stimulation technique that applies magnetic pulses to the prefrontal cortex. FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression. Shows response rates of 50–60% in people who have not responded to antidepressants.
strategies.notes: No systemic side effects. Requires daily sessions over 4–6 weeks. Increasingly available; costs remain a barrier without insurance coverage.

things_to_avoid: Alcohol — a central nervous system depressant that worsens mood over time and disrupts sleep architecture; used as self-medication by many people with depression, it reliably makes the condition worse
Prolonged social isolation — the behavioural withdrawal driven by depression is self-reinforcing; avoiding people worsens long-term outcomes even when contact feels temporarily easier to avoid
Excessive sleep — while rest is important, sleeping beyond 8–9 hours or napping extensively through the day disrupts circadian rhythm and perpetuates low mood and fatigue

Starting antidepressants without investigating potential reversible physical causes — thyroid dysfunction, anaemia, vitamin D deficiency, and hormonal imbalances can all be corrected, and missing them means treating a symptom while the driver continues
Abruptly stopping antidepressants — discontinuation syndrome can be severe and mistaken for relapse; a slow taper under supervision is essential

Ultra-processed, high-sugar diets — consistently associated with worse depression outcomes across large prospective studies; a dietary overhaul is not required but reducing processed food meaningfully matters
Caffeine in excess — can worsen anxiety that co-occurs with depression, disrupt sleep, and increase cortisol; affects individuals differently

Dismissing physical symptoms as 'just depression' — depression produces real physical symptoms and can also co-occur with physical illness; physical complaints in depressed individuals should still be investigated rather than automatically attributed to mood

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Antidepressants are the beginning of treatment, not the solution. For many people, they reduce symptoms enough to function but do not resolve the underlying drivers. Stopping investigation once a prescription is written is one of the most common reasons people remain chronically unwell.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Getting a prescription feels like a problem solved. It requires less time, effort, and confrontation than investigating lifestyle, trauma history, or biological root causes. Both patients and prescribers often accept partial improvement as adequate.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who use the medication as a floor — giving them enough stability to do the harder investigative and lifestyle work — tend to achieve substantially better long-term outcomes than those who use it as a ceiling.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Waiting to feel motivated before making changes is how depression keeps people stuck. Depression specifically attacks motivation and prediction of positive outcomes — waiting for it to lift before acting is physiologically backwards.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The subjective experience of depression makes action feel pointless. The idea that feeling should come before action is deeply intuitive but precisely wrong in this condition.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who act first — in the smallest manageable increments — and wait for mood to follow (rather than the reverse) consistently describe this as the turning point. The brain updates its predictions based on actual experience, not on intention.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Unresolved trauma is a major driver of depression that talking about symptoms without addressing does not fix. Processing the trauma — not just managing its symptoms — is often the difference between chronic management and genuine recovery.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Trauma processing is painful, effortful, and requires a skilled therapist. Many people prefer symptom management to reprocessing painful memories. Trauma is also frequently not identified as the driver in standard care.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Trauma-focused therapies (EMDR, somatic therapy, trauma-informed CBT, or IFS) used with a skilled practitioner produce durable outcomes for many people with trauma-driven depression who have found conventional depression treatment inadequate.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A diagnosis of 'depression' is a description, not an explanation. Two people with identical PHQ-9 scores may have completely different biological and psychological drivers. Treatment that doesn't investigate the driver is systematically limited.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The diagnostic label feels like an answer. Investigating further — hormones, inflammation, gut health, sleep, trauma, metabolic factors — requires effort, often personal expense, and may not be supported by the prescribing clinician.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who approach their depression as a pattern requiring explanation — rather than a condition requiring management — and who persist in investigating root causes, have the highest rates of substantive and lasting improvement.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Social isolation makes depression worse, but depression makes social contact feel impossible. There is no comfortable way through this — re-engagement with people typically has to happen before it feels wanted.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Depression generates convincing thoughts that others don't want contact, that reaching out is a burden, or that interactions will be draining. These feel like accurate perceptions but are symptoms of the condition.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who establish small, low-stakes regular social contact — structured so that cancelling is harder than attending — consistently report this as one of the highest-impact changes they made, even though initiating it felt terrible.

key_levers.lever: Identify and address reversible physical drivers
key_levers.why_it_matters: Thyroid dysfunction, nutritional deficiencies (B12, vitamin D, iron, folate), hormonal dysregulation, and inflammatory conditions can produce or significantly worsen depression. Treating the driver, not the symptom, produces durable change that medication alone cannot replicate.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a comprehensive blood panel including full thyroid function, vitamin D, B12, folate, ferritin, hs-CRP, and fasting glucose. If hormonal symptoms are present, add a sex hormone panel. Work with a practitioner willing to interpret results in the context of symptoms, not only against standard reference ranges.
key_levers.timeline: 4–12 weeks to see meaningful change once identified deficiencies are corrected

key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise is the single most robustly evidenced lifestyle intervention for depression, with effect sizes comparable to antidepressants for mild-to-moderate severity. Its effects are multi-mechanistic: BDNF upregulation, HPA axis normalisation, anti-inflammatory action, sleep improvement, and social benefit if done in groups.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with 10-minute walks every day for two weeks. Add five minutes per week. Aim for 150 minutes of moderate activity weekly. Scheduling it at the same time each day substantially improves adherence.
key_levers.timeline: Mood benefits often detectable within 2–4 weeks; full effect over 8–12 weeks

key_levers.lever: Process underlying trauma with a qualified therapist
key_levers.why_it_matters: Unprocessed trauma keeps the nervous system in a dysregulated state that perpetuates depression regardless of medication or lifestyle interventions. EMDR and somatic approaches process trauma at a neurobiological level that cognitive discussion alone does not reach.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a therapist specifically trained in EMDR, somatic experiencing, or trauma-focused CBT. General-purpose counselling is not equivalent. Trauma treatment requires a practitioner with specific training.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful shift often occurs within 8–20 sessions, though complex trauma requires longer engagement

key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep disruption is both a symptom and a cause of depression in a self-reinforcing cycle. Fixing sleep timing — even before sleep quality improves — stabilises mood regulation, reduces cortisol dysregulation, and increases response to other interventions.
key_levers.how_to_start: Set a consistent wake time and hold it regardless of sleep quality. Get outside or near a bright window within 30 minutes of waking. Reduce artificial light after dark. Avoid caffeine after noon.
key_levers.timeline: Circadian stabilisation begins within 1–2 weeks; mood benefit builds over 4–8 weeks

key_levers.why_it_matters: Diet quality is consistently associated with depression outcomes in large prospective studies and multiple randomised trials. The gut microbiome, inflammation, and nutrient availability all mediate this relationship. Improving diet is a meaningful lever even when everything else is in place.
key_levers.how_to_start: Reduce ultra-processed foods and added sugar as a first step. Add oily fish twice weekly, increase colourful vegetables to two or more servings per meal, and consider a Mediterranean eating pattern as a framework.
key_levers.timeline: Gut microbiome changes begin within 2–4 weeks; mood benefit measurable at 12 weeks in trial data

key_levers.lever: Engage in evidence-based psychological therapy (CBT or MBCT)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Psychological therapy addresses the cognitive and behavioural patterns that maintain depression. Unlike medication, well-delivered therapy produces durable change — reducing relapse rates long after treatment ends. It is most effective combined with other levers.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a CBT-trained therapist via a GP referral, IAPT (UK), or private directory. For relapse prevention, MBCT is particularly effective and is available in group format. iCBT programmes are a meaningful option when in-person access is limited.
key_levers.timeline: Initial benefit often within 4–8 sessions; full course typically 12–20 sessions

common_mistakes: Accepting partial improvement on antidepressants as the goal, without investigating why full remission was not achieved — partial response is common but should prompt further investigation, not indefinite maintenance on an inadequate dose or drug
Treating depression in isolation without addressing co-occurring anxiety, ADHD, or trauma — untreated comorbidities reliably prevent full recovery
Switching antidepressants repeatedly without investigating root causes — pharmacogenomic testing and root-cause investigation are more rational than continued sequential prescribing in non-responders
Using alcohol or cannabis to manage symptoms — both worsen depression over time, disrupt sleep, and create dependency risk; they are among the most common and most damaging self-medication strategies

Expecting consistent linear progress — depression recovery is non-linear, with good days triggering overactivity and setbacks triggering catastrophising; expecting fluctuation prevents these misinterpretations from derailing treatment

Stopping antidepressants abruptly or shortly after feeling better — relapse risk is highest in the first six months after symptom remission; standard guidance is to continue for at least 6–12 months after recovery in a first episode
Attributing all physical symptoms to depression without investigation — depression can be secondary to physical illness, and physical symptoms in depressed people still deserve clinical assessment

specialists.role: Provides expert diagnostic assessment — particularly important for ruling out bipolar disorder, ADHD, or psychotic features — and manages complex medication decisions including treatment-resistant cases, augmentation strategies, and pharmacogenomic-guided prescribing.
specialists.notes: Most useful for moderate-to-severe, treatment-resistant, or diagnostically complex cases. Access via GP referral in most public health systems. Private access is faster but expensive.

specialists.role: Delivers evidence-based psychological therapies (CBT, MBCT, ACT, schema therapy) that produce durable change. Essential for anyone whose depression has psychological, trauma, or behavioural maintenance factors — which is most people.
specialists.notes: Look for specific training in the therapy type needed. Trauma history warrants a trauma-trained therapist specifically. General counselling is not equivalent to structured CBT or trauma-focused approaches.

specialists.role: Processes underlying trauma that drives or maintains depression at a neurobiological level. Particularly important in people with adverse childhood experiences, PTSD, or complex trauma where standard depression treatment has been inadequate.
specialists.notes: EMDR has a strong evidence base for trauma-related depression. Somatic approaches (SE, sensorimotor psychotherapy) are particularly useful where trauma is stored in the body. Look for accreditation with EMDR Association or equivalent.

specialists.type: Functional medicine or integrative medicine doctor
specialists.role: Investigates biological drivers of depression including thyroid dysfunction, nutritional deficiencies, hormonal imbalances, inflammatory markers, gut health, and metabolic factors. Tends to order more comprehensive panels than mainstream GPs and interpret results in the context of symptoms.
specialists.notes: Quality varies considerably. Look for practitioners with formal functional medicine training (e.g. IFMCP credential). Many mainstream doctors are sceptical of this approach, but for treatment-resistant cases it can identify reversible causes that standard care missed.

specialists.role: Investigates and treats hormonal contributors to depression — particularly thyroid disorders, cortisol dysregulation (Cushing's or adrenal insufficiency), and reproductive hormone imbalances.
specialists.notes: More appropriate than a GP for complex thyroid cases or suspected cortisol abnormalities. May not investigate sex hormones in the context of depression without prompting.

specialists.role: For women where depression is tied to the menstrual cycle (PMDD), perimenopause, postpartum changes, or endometriosis — hormonal investigation and management is central and beyond the scope of most GPs.
specialists.notes: Menopause specialists (BMS-accredited in the UK, NAMS in the US) are often better equipped for perimenopausal depression than general gynaecologists.

specialists.role: First point of contact for initial assessment, blood test orders, and referrals. Can manage mild-to-moderate depression with medication and basic psychosocial advice.
specialists.notes: Many GPs are time-limited and trained primarily in symptom management rather than root-cause investigation. Bringing a list of specific test requests (full thyroid panel, ferritin, vitamin D, hs-CRP, etc.) significantly improves the quality of the assessment.

patient_communities.name: r/depression
patient_communities.notes: A large peer support community with a focus on emotional support and shared experience. Useful for reducing isolation. Moderated to maintain a safe space; not focused on medical investigation.

patient_communities.name: r/TreatmentResistantDepression
patient_communities.notes: Specifically for people who have not responded to standard treatments. Contains detailed discussions of ketamine, TMS, ECT, augmentation strategies, pharmacogenomics, and root-cause approaches. More medically detailed than general depression communities.

patient_communities.name: r/depressionregimens
patient_communities.notes: Focuses on treatment protocols, supplement stacks, and evidence review. Attracts a more research-oriented audience. Useful for people investigating biological contributors and non-standard interventions.

patient_communities.name: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA)
patient_communities.notes: US-based peer support organisation with online and in-person support groups. Clinically informed. Particularly valuable for people navigating complex mood disorders.

patient_communities.name: Mind Community Forums
patient_communities.notes: UK-based mental health charity with community forums and detailed information resources. Useful for navigating NHS treatment options and peer support.

learning_resources.title: Lost Connections: Why You're Depressed and How to Find Hope
learning_resources.description: A well-researched and accessible examination of the social, psychological, and environmental causes of depression that standard treatment often ignores. Challenges the serotonin deficiency narrative and explores what research actually shows about causes and solutions. Widely read and a useful starting point for people who feel their depression has not been adequately explained.

learning_resources.title: The Depression Cure
learning_resources.description: Based on Ilardi's Therapeutic Lifestyle Change (TLC) programme, this book outlines six lifestyle-based interventions with strong research support: exercise, omega-3s, light exposure, social engagement, meaningful activity, and sleep. Practical, evidence-based, and written for a general audience.

learning_resources.title: Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy
learning_resources.description: The foundational self-help CBT book, with decades of evidence for reducing depressive symptoms. Used in clinical settings as a bibliotherapy tool. Covers cognitive distortions, behavioural activation, and relapse prevention in a structured, practical format.

learning_resources.title: The Body Keeps the Score
learning_resources.description: The definitive work on trauma's effects on the body and brain, and how unprocessed trauma drives chronic mental health conditions including depression. Essential reading for anyone whose depression has roots in adverse experiences. Also covers effective trauma treatments.

learning_resources.title: Inflamed: Deep Medicine and the Anatomy of Injustice
learning_resources.description: Explores the intersection of systemic inflammation, chronic illness, and social determinants of health. Useful for understanding the inflammatory model of depression in a broader social context.

learning_resources.title: SMILES Trial (Supporting the Modification of Lifestyle in Lowered Emotional States)
learning_resources.description: The landmark randomised controlled trial demonstrating that dietary improvement produces clinically meaningful reductions in depression scores. Foundational reading for understanding the diet-depression relationship and often cited in nutrition-psychiatry discussions.

learning_resources.title: Huberman Lab — Understanding Depression
learning_resources.description: A detailed, neuroscience-informed episode covering the mechanisms of depression, evidence-based interventions including light therapy, exercise, and supplements, and an honest assessment of the antidepressant literature. Well-suited to people who want to understand the biology.

learning_resources.title: National Institute of Mental Health — Depression
learning_resources.description: Clear, authoritative information on diagnostic criteria, treatment options, and research updates. A reliable reference point for understanding the standard clinical landscape of depression.

learning_resources.title: The Mindful Way Through Depression
learning_resources.description: Written by the developers of MBCT, this book introduces the approach for preventing depressive relapse through mindfulness. Includes guided meditations and exercises. Particularly valuable for people with recurrent depression.

sections.relevance_signal: **Gut symptoms alongside low mood, low energy, or loss of interest**. Constipation dominant. Symptoms are chronic and pervasive without clear dietary triggers.
